old_claim.note: Hospital Patient Wards provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Pilgrimage route services depended on hospitality and care institutions for travelers; medieval route evidence specifically names hospitals, hospices, food, and lodging for pilgrims.

source_shape.source_locator: Outstanding Universal Value identifies hospitals and old pilgrim hospitals as specialized edifices meeting the spiritual and physical well-being of medieval pilgrims.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: UNESCO identifies hospitals and old pilgrim hospitals as part of medieval Santiago-route infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports charitable hospital and hospice networks as an institutional substrate for route services. It is narrower and more relevant than generic patient-ward organization.

ontology_before: Pilgrimage Route Services depended on Hospital Patient Wards, implying ward organization was the direct prerequisite for pilgrim route hospitality.
ontology_after: Pilgrimage Route Services depends on Charitable Hospital Networks, which explicitly covers shelter and organized care for travelers, poor people, and the sick.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: pilgrimage_route_services now depends on charitable_hospital_networks and no longer directly depends on hospital_patient_wards.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to medical ward management for sick pilgrims rather than pilgrimage route support.
A source showed hospital ward layout was the direct enabling technology for pilgrimage route services.
The charitable_hospital_networks node were narrowed so it no longer covered shelter and organized care for travelers.
short_reason: Pilgrimage hospitality is better represented by charitable hospital networks than by generic patient wards.
